metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the canonical SHA format (full 40-char or short N-char) for `COMMIT` across both the env-var path and the git-fallback path, and normalize in `hatch_build.py` so developer and release installs always bake the same length; update the integration test to assert the full-SHA case.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT=${{ github.sha }}` (always a full 40-char SHA) as the primary commit source, while the existing git-fallback path calls `git rev-parse --short HEAD` (7-8 chars). The plan never specifies which length is canonical or normalizes between the two sources, so the wheel bakes a full SHA in release builds and a short SHA on developer installs — a silent behavioural difference. Downstream code in `signing.py` that displays or pattern-matches the commit will see mismatched formats, and the AC's integration test uses `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT=deadbeef` (short), which doesn't exercise the full-SHA case.
findings.location: 'Fix the hook' section — hatch_build.py / `_build_commit()` + AC #3 (integration test)
findings.suggested_fix: In `hatch_build.py`, after reading from either source, normalize to a consistent length — e.g. always use the full SHA: change the git fallback from `git rev-parse --short HEAD` to `git rev-parse HEAD` (or strip/expand consistently). Document the canonical length in a comment and update the AC integration test to use a full 40-char hex string.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` carries a full or short SHA (and align `github.sha` usage accordingly), and update `hatch_build.py`'s git fallback to produce the same format — so `_build_info.COMMIT` is consistent across release and dev installs and AC #3's equality assertion is satisfiable.
findings.finding: The plan sets `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT=${{ github.sha }}` (GitHub's 40-character full SHA) as the env var, but the current `hatch_build.py` `_build_commit()` function returns a *short* SHA via `git rev-parse --short HEAD` (6–8 hex chars). If `initialize()` simply substitutes the env var value for the git call result, `_build_info.COMMIT` will carry a full SHA in release wheels but a short SHA in dev installs — an inconsistent format across paths. AC #3 requires 'assert the wheel contains COMMIT equal to that SHA,' which would always fail for the git-fallback path if 'that SHA' means the full 40-char form. The existing test at line 227 also asserts `COMMIT = '[0-9a-f]{4,}'` (matches both), but any equality check against `github.sha` would fail dev builds.
findings.location: ## What — 'Fix the hook' bullet; hatch_build.py `_build_commit()`; AC #3
findings.suggested_fix: Use `${{ github.sha }}` as-is (full SHA) and update `_build_commit()` to call `git rev-parse HEAD` (drop `--short`) so the git-fallback path also returns a full 40-char SHA. Update the existing regex assertion in test_attestation_signing.py:227 accordingly (`[0-9a-f]{40}`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that reference SHA-256 hashes are stored OUT-OF-BAND from the artifact bundle (e.g., as a signed GitHub Actions job output or a separate attestation artifact) so the publish-job hash comparison is not circular.
findings.finding: The plan says the publish job 'recomputes and compares hashes against the recorded build hashes' but never specifies WHERE the reference hashes are stored or how they are passed to the publish job. If the reference hashes live inside the same artifact bundle being verified, the check is circular (the artifact verifies itself) and provides no integrity guarantee. They must be stored out-of-band (e.g., as a signed workflow output, a separate attestation artifact, or a job output variable) to be meaningful.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — first bullet; What section — 'Publish the same bytes' paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: In the build job, emit the computed hashes as a GitHub Actions `outputs` variable (or upload a separate, independently named attestation artifact). The publish job downloads both the artifact bundle and the out-of-band hashes, recomputes, and compares. This ensures a tampered or swapped bundle is detected.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Merge AC #3 and AC #4 into a single criterion (or explicitly distinguish them: e.g., AC #3 = the workflow/pipeline assertion that the shipped wheel has the correct SHA; AC #4 = the unit/integration test in `test_attestation_signing.py` that covers the sdist→wheel code path), so an implementer knows exactly how many tests are required and what each must assert.
findings.finding: AC #3 and AC #4 are near-duplicate requirements: both specify a test that exercises the wheel-from-sdist build path and asserts the baked commit is non-null. AC #3 says 'a test asserts the wheel built via the default sdist→wheel path contains COMMIT equal to that SHA (today this path yields COMMIT = None)'; AC #4 says 'A test exercises the wheel-from-sdist build path and asserts the baked commit is non-null.' An implementer cannot tell whether these are meant to be one test or two distinct tests with different assertions.
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite AC #3 to cover the workflow-level assertion (the release pipeline produces a wheel with COMMIT matching github.sha) and AC #4 to cover the code-level test (a test in test_attestation_signing.py or similar that runs REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT=deadbeef python -m build and asserts the extracted wheel contains COMMIT='deadbeef'), making the two criteria distinct in scope and verifiable independently.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (by reading pypa/build source or running the proposed integration test pre-merge) that `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` set in the host environment IS inherited by the Hatchling backend subprocess when `python -m build` runs with default build isolation; if it is not, switch to `python -m build --no-isolation` (flag: `--no-isolation`, not `--no-build-isolation`) or use a pyproject.toml `[tool.hatch.build]` config entry that captures the SHA before the isolation boundary.
findings.finding: The plan asserts as settled fact that 'pypa/build forwards host env vars into the Hatchling backend subprocess through the sdist→wheel path (it does, via subprocess.run with inherited env)' — but this claim is unverifiable from the repo's file tools (the installed build package's source is inaccessible), and it conflates two distinct behaviors: (a) subprocess.run's default env-inheritance, and (b) whether pypa/build's build-isolation layer (the default sandboxed venv it creates for each build backend invocation) propagates the caller's full env or strips/sanitises it. Default `python -m build` uses PEP 517 isolated builds; whether REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT set in the outer shell survives into the Hatchling hook's os.environ inside the isolated backend subprocess is a library-implementation detail the plan asserts without evidence. If the isolation layer does NOT pass through arbitrary host env vars, the entire env-var hook-fix strategy fails silently (the wheel is built from the sdist with REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT absent, hook falls back to git rev-parse, finds no .git, writes COMMIT = None — exactly the bug being fixed).
findings.location: What section — 'Fix the hook' subsection: 'Integration verification: confirm `pypa/build` forwards host env vars into the Hatchling backend subprocess through the sdist→wheel path (it does, via `subprocess.run` with inherited env)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add the integration test (`REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT=deadbeef python -m build && unzip -p dist/*.whl rebar/_build_info.py | grep deadbeef`) as a pre-merge check in a throwaway CI step or a pytest test that actually invokes `subprocess.run(['python', '-m', 'build', ...], env={**os.environ, 'REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT': 'deadbeef'})` and inspects the wheel's COMMIT — this proves or refutes the claim before the plan is committed to the env-var approach.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before committing to the env-var injection approach, explicitly verify (or cite a primary source confirming) that `pypa/build` in its default isolated mode forwards the calling process's env vars — specifically a custom var like `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` — into the Hatchling backend subprocess for the wheel-from-extracted-sdist second stage; if the verification fails, specify the fallback (e.g., `--no-isolation`, bake SHA into sdist metadata, or the preserve-existing-_build_info.py approach).
findings.finding: The plan's core fix mechanism relies on an unverified capability assertion: that `pypa/build` (in its default isolated mode) propagates the host shell's `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` env var into the Hatchling backend subprocess for the second-stage wheel-from-extracted-sdist build. The plan parenthetically claims this 'does' work 'via subprocess.run with inherited env', but this is not trivially true in isolated mode — pypa/build creates a fresh venv and may or may not filter env vars in the subprocess chain. If this assertion is wrong, the entire env-var fix architecture must be replaced (e.g., with --no-isolation, the 'preserve existing _build_info.py' approach, or an sdist-post-processing step). The plan schedules an integration test to confirm this but treats the outcome as already known, creating a design commitment before verification.
findings.location: What section — 'Fix the hook (decision: inject the SHA via an env var)' and 'Integration verification' paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line verification step before the plan's integration test: run `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT=probe python -m build 2>&1 | grep -q probe || echo FAIL` against the actual installed version of pypa/build in the dev environment to confirm env propagation, and document the result as a confirmed fact. If it fails, pivot to `python -m build --no-isolation` (with a note that this requires the build deps to be pre-installed) or the alternative sdist-bakes-SHA approach as the fallback.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the mandated `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT=deadbeef python -m build` integration test to the test suite *before* merging (not as a follow-on), so the env-var-forwarding assumption is verified by a runnable gate rather than stated as fact.
findings.finding: The plan's central fix relies on `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` surviving from the workflow shell through `python -m build`'s PEP 517 isolated build into the Hatchling hook subprocess during the sdist→wheel stage. The plan states this 'does' happen 'via `subprocess.run` with inherited env,' but this is an assertion based on general knowledge, not an observed flag/API probe. pypa/build's isolated mode creates a clean venv but does inherit the parent env — however, this is an implementation detail that could change. The plan mandates a confirming integration test, which is the right call, but the test is currently absent and the feasibility assertion is Partially-verified, not Observed.
findings.location: ## What — 'Fix the hook' bullet, 'Integration verification' paragraph

findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: The plan's hook-fix strategy relies on an env var set by the workflow propagating into the Hatchling `initialize()` hook when `python -m build` builds the wheel from the extracted sdist, but this env-var forwarding path through pypa/build's PEP 517 isolation layer is asserted, not probed. pypa/build does forward the parent's environment by default (unlike strict sandbox isolation), but the plan itself signals the mechanism is undecided ('e.g. an env var the workflow sets') and the alternative ('preserve an existing non-null `_build_info.py`') depends on the sdist carrying `_build_info.py` — yet the current `pyproject.toml` `[tool.hatch.build.targets.sdist]` `include` list covers `src/rebar` generically and `_build_info.py` is git-ignored, making it plausible but unverified that the sdist includes it. If the env-var approach is chosen and pypa/build does NOT forward it (e.g. a future pypa/build version changes behavior, or `--no-build-isolation` is required), the hook silently falls back to `COMMIT = None` — reproducing the original defect even after this fix ships.
findings.location: ## What — 'Fix the hook' bullet; hatch_build.py; pyproject.toml [tool.hatch.build.targets.sdist]
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-paragraph 'Integration verification' note under the 'Fix the hook' bullet: (1) confirm pypa/build forwards host env vars into the Hatchling hook subprocess by running `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT=test python -m build` in a temp dir without `.git` and asserting the wheel's `_build_info.py` contains `COMMIT = 'test'`; (2) if that passes, name the env var explicitly (e.g. `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT`); (3) if it does not, use the sdist-carry approach and add `src/rebar/_build_info.py` to an explicit `artifacts` or `force-include` in the sdist config to ensure it ships.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the `github_release` job in the Scope section and specify whether SHA-256 manifest files (or any new artifact bundle members) should or should not be attached to the GitHub Release, and update its `dist/*` glob or `needs:` accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan restructures the release.yml artifact bundle and the publish job's `needs:` chain, but does not enumerate the `github_release` job as a consumer requiring coordinated review. That job downloads `name: dist` (release.yml line 87) and attaches its contents to the GitHub Release. If the plan adds SHA-256 sidecar files to the bundle, renames the artifact, or changes the bundle structure, the `github_release` job will upload those extra/renamed files to the GitHub Release without author awareness.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `github_release` to the Scope section with a note: 'verify that the github_release job's `dist/*` upload glob still produces the correct Release asset list (wheel + sdist only, no SHA manifest) after the artifact bundle is restructured; exclude or filter hash files if needed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a unit test in `tests/unit/test_attestation_signing.py` (or a dedicated hook test) that sets `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` to an empty string (and separately to a non-resolvable value) and asserts `hatch_build.py`'s `initialize` raises, confirming the fail-fast branch is exercised and cannot silently regress.
findings.finding: The new fail-fast branch in hatch_build.py — where REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT is set but empty or unresolvable and the hook raises — has no test specified anywhere in the plan. The plan specifies tests for the happy path (COMMIT non-null in the built wheel) and implicitly covers the graceful fallback (env var unset → git), but the raise path is new user-facing behavior with no corresponding test.
findings.location: Scope › hatch_build.py; AC bullet 3 ('The hook fails the build only when REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT is set but unresolvable'); What › Fix the hook (decision: inject the SHA via an env var)
findings.suggested_fix:
Add a parametrized pytest case:
```python
@pytest.mark.parametrize("bad_val", ["", "  "])
def test_hook_raises_when_build_commit_env_var_is_empty(bad_val, monkeypatch):
    monkeypatch.setenv("REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT", bad_val)
    with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT"):
        # call initialize() directly, bypassing git
        builder = WheelBuilder(...)
        builder.initialize(...)
```
Also add an AC bullet: 'A test asserts the hook raises when `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` is set to an empty or whitespace-only value.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief note to `CLAUDE.md` (or a lightweight ADR) documenting the `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` env-var contract — its role as the primary SHA source, the fail-fast scope in release context, and the git-fallback for dev installs — so the decision is discoverable without reading `docs/releasing.md`.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new developer-facing build contract (`REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` env var as primary commit-SHA source in hatch_build.py) and a novel architectural pattern (build-once with env-var injection), but scopes documentation only to `docs/releasing.md`. Developer-oriented docs (CLAUDE.md, CONTRIBUTING.md, or a brief ADR) are not mentioned, leaving the contract undiscoverable for contributors who modify hatch_build.py or the CI workflows without reading the release doc.
findings.location: Scope section / docs/releasing.md bullet; hatch_build.py bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Build backend contract' section to CLAUDE.md (or a docs/adr/build-once.md) covering: (1) REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT is set by release.yml before python -m build; (2) hatch_build.py reads it first, falls back to git rev-parse when unset; (3) fail-fast (raise) only when the var is set-but-empty (release context). One paragraph suffices; it need not duplicate the full rationale in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `.github/workflows/_optionality.yml` to the consumer acknowledgment in the Scope section, confirming that its `python -m build --wheel` call (without `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` set) will continue to use the git-fallback path and not break.
findings.finding: The `.github/workflows/_optionality.yml` workflow runs `python -m build --wheel` (line 90) directly — invoking hatch_build.py — and is an unenumerated consumer of the hook changes. The plan does not acknowledge this workflow as a site that calls the build hook, nor confirm that the unset-REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT path in the modified hook remains safe when run in this CI context (which does have a .git checkout, so the git fallback will fire, but this is not stated).

AC2 lists multiple named probes ('CLI/entry-point/import/MCP-init/packaged-resource probes') for both the wheel-test and sdist-test jobs without specifying the concrete commands or assertions that would prove each probe passes; an executor cannot determine whether the probe list is exhaustive or what 'passing' looks like for 'packaged-resource checks' and 'MCP init' specifically.

The 'Fix the hook' mechanism is presented as an unresolved OR ('pass the trusted source SHA into the build as an explicit input (e.g. an env var the workflow sets), or preserve an existing non-null _build_info.py') without selecting one approach and without stating positive rationale for the chosen mechanism. The two branches have different correctness implications: the env-var branch depends on Hatchling's isolated wheel build inheriting the parent process environment (true for subprocess-based PEP 517 isolation, but not validated in the plan), while the preserve-existing branch depends on the sdist containing a non-null _build_info.py and the hook checking before overwriting (currently the hook writes unconditionally). An implementer who picks the wrong branch, or misunderstands the ordering dependency, reproduces the original defect silently.
findings.location: ## What — 'Fix the hook' bullet; ## Scope — hatch_build.py
findings.suggested_fix: Choose the env-var branch (it's simpler and auditable): name the variable `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT`, have the workflow set it to `${{ github.sha }}` before `python -m build`, update `hatch_build.py` to read `os.environ.get('REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT')` as the primary source and `git rev-parse` as the fallback, and fail if both are absent during a release build (detected by checking whether `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` is set). Confirm that Hatchling's default isolation passes parent env vars to the backend subprocess (it does, via `subprocess.run` with inherited env).

findings.finding: AC2 names 'MCP init' as a required probe in the wheel-test job, but no CLI subcommand, script, or entry-point named 'mcp init' (or 'rebar mcp-init', 'rebar-mcp init', etc.) exists in the repo. The closest analog is `rebar-mcp --help` (already listed as a separate probe).

The 'Fix the hook' implementation strategy is presented as an unranked OR choice — 'pass the trusted source SHA into the build as an explicit input (e.g. an env var the workflow sets), OR preserve an existing non-null _build_info.py when rebuilding from an sdist without git metadata' — leaving a real design decision (two architecturally distinct approaches with different trade-offs) to the executor without a stated default.
findings.location: ## What — 'Fix the hook' bullet; ## Scope — hatch_build.py bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the OR clause with a single stated default: 'Inject the release SHA via a `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` environment variable set in the workflow; `hatch_build.py` reads this var first, falls back to `git rev-parse` if `.git` is present, and raises a hard error if neither resolves.' Then add `REBAR_BUILD_COMMIT` to the pyproject.toml env-var list if Hatchling supports it.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
